name: streaming_sse_v1
description: Server-Sent Events streaming inference dataset. Covers SSE protocol mechanics (event framing, keepalive, connection lifecycle), OpenAI chat.completion.chunk compatibility (field mapping, finish_reason, model naming), backpressure handling (64KB buffer, generation pause/resume), client disconnect cleanup (KV cache freeing, metric updates), and per-token adapter routing visibility in stream metadata. 1500 examples with production-grade technical depth.

entries.notes: 1500 streaming SSE examples across five subcategories: SSE protocol (300), OpenAI compatibility (300), backpressure handling (300), client disconnect (300), streaming with adapter routing (300)

provenance.review_notes: Synthetic dataset with production-accurate SSE framing, OpenAI-compatible chunk formats, backpressure scenarios, disconnect cleanup sequences, and per-token adapter routing metadata. All protocol details derived from the adapterOS streaming_infer.rs and openai_compat.rs handlers.

evaluation_gates: SSE events use correct data: prefix and double-newline termination
Keepalive comments use colon-space prefix (: keepalive) not data: prefix
OpenAI chunk format includes id, object=chat.completion.chunk, created, model, choices
finish_reason is null for content chunks and 'stop'/'length' for terminal chunks
Backpressure threshold correctly identified as 64KB buffer
Client disconnect triggers KV cache cleanup and generation cancellation
Adapter routing metadata shows per-token adapter selection in stream chunks
Final event is always data: [DONE] followed by double newline
Heartbeat interval is 15 seconds for connection keep-alive
system_fingerprint contains BLAKE3 receipt digest for determinism tracking
intent: Train the adapter to reason about adapterOS streaming inference: SSE protocol correctness (event framing, keepalive, reconnection), OpenAI API compatibility (chunk format, field semantics, finish reasons), server-side flow control (backpressure, buffering, pause/resume), connection lifecycle management (disconnect detection, resource cleanup), and the unique adapterOS feature of per-token adapter routing visibility in streaming responses.
